title: Scalar and Vector Airborne Platform Calibration Using Quantum and Classical Magnetometers and Inertial Sensors
authors: Antonia Hager, Torleiv H. Bryne, Mia Jukić
abstract: Airborne magnetometry requires rigorous calibration to isolate geomagnetic signals from sensor errors and platform magnetic fields. This magnetic compensation is needed for applications like geophysical exploration and magnetic anomaly navigation. The standard approach utilizes a quantum scalar Optically Pumped Magnetometer (OPM) and a less sensitive fluxgate vector sensor for attitude information. This configuration typically results in a scalar approximation of the platform field.   Advancements in high-sensitivity Diamond Nitrogen-Vacancy (NV) vector magnetometers now enable a re-evaluation of the standard hardware configuration and full vector calibration models. We show through rigorous theoretical analysis that scalar calibration models are robust to misalignment. Vector calibration models, however, are intrinsically first-order sensitive to attitude errors, irrespective of the accuracy of the magnetic field measurements. These errors arise from inaccurate representation of the background field direction in the body frame, and can amplify small orientation errors into noticeable calibration residuals.   Using realistic sensor models and flight trajectories, we evaluate different sensor configurations for magnetic calibration and assess the use of onboard Inertial Navigation Systems (INS) as an independent attitude reference to enable stable compensation. Our results suggest that quantum vector magnetometers like NV sensors are not sufficient to solve the attitude bottleneck for airborne vector magnetic calibration. However, as a single sensor capable of providing both absolute field and directional measurements, they may offer benefits regarding sensor placement, synchronization, and scalar calibration accuracy.
summary: This paper evaluates how different magnetometer calibration models perform under realistic airborne conditions. It demonstrates that while 3D vector models are highly sensitive to orientation errors, 1D scalar models are much more robust, and it highlights the potential for NV sensors to improve sensor integration in magnetic navigation.
main_problem: The paper investigates the fundamental error sources in airborne magnetic platform calibration, specifically comparing the robustness of 1D (scalar) versus 3D (vector) Tolles-Lawson calibration models against attitude and sensor errors.
main_result: The 1D scalar model is found to be highly robust to attitude errors, whereas the 3D vector model is intrinsically sensitive to first-order attitude inaccuracies. While NV sensors may not solve the 'attitude bottleneck,' they offer advantages in sensor integration and synchronization.
method: The authors use a rigorous theoretical analysis of error dependencies combined with a simulation framework that employs realistic sensor noise models, frequency-domain noise synthesis, and simulated aircraft flight trajectories.
model_or_system: The system is an airborne platform (aircraft/UAV) equipped with various magnetometers (OPM, Fluxgate, and NV diamond sensors) and an Inertial Navigation System (INS). The magnetic interference is modeled using the Tolles-Lawson model, accounting for permanent, induced, and eddy-current components.
key_observables: Calibration residuals (error in nT), total magnetic field, Earth's magnetic field, and attitude/angular errors.
important_parameters: Earth's field magnitude (50,000 nT), platform interference field, angular error (alpha), and sensor noise parameters (white noise floor, corner frequency, and spectral slope).
main_assumptions: The study focuses on static platform field components and ignores time-varying electronic/temperature-induced nonlinearities; it also assumes a constant, uniform geomagnetic background field.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the impact of field angle on projection errors; Figure 3/4 detail the sensor noise and error generation pipeline; Figure 6 shows sensor stability (ASD/ADEV); Figure 7/12 illustrate simulated flight trajectories and calibration/validation results.
paper_structure: The paper begins with a theoretical derivation of error dependencies in scalar and vector models, followed by the description of the sensor and noise models, the simulation framework for flight trajectories, a comparison of calibration results, and a discussion of the utility of NV sensors.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
